title: Morse Code Alphabet and Numbers
summary: The complete International Morse Code character table (A–Z and 0–9) as standardized by ITU-R M.1677-1, with timing rules and tips for using Morse code to signal in an emergency.
quick_answer: SOS in Morse code is · · · − − − · · · (three dots, three dashes, three dots). Tap, flash, or blast this pattern to signal distress when other communication is unavailable.
when_to_use: When you need to look up a specific letter or number to encode a message in Morse code
When learning how to send the SOS distress signal before an emergency
When communicating with rescuers using a flashlight, whistle, mirror, or tapping on a surface
When cell service, radio, and voice communication are unavailable or ineffective
When you need to signal across distances that voice cannot carry
preferred_action: Use the SOS pattern (· · · − − − · · ·) as your default distress signal — it is universally recognized and does not require the receiver to know Morse code.
backup_action: If you cannot remember SOS, three of anything repeated (three flashes, three blasts, three fires, three rocks) is a recognized distress signal in many rescue contexts.
warnings: Sending incorrect Morse signals may confuse rescuers or obscure your distress message.
Spending excessive time encoding messages instead of seeking shelter, warmth, or water can worsen your survival situation.
Morse code is only useful if someone is watching or listening — make sure rescuers are in range before prolonged signaling.
In low-light conditions, a flashlight battery drains quickly; use short, efficient SOS bursts rather than extended transmissions.
what_not_to_do: Do not insert pauses between the S, O, and S groups when sending SOS — it is one continuous prosign, not three separate letters.
Do not use random flashing or tapping without a pattern — rescuers may not recognize it as a distress signal.
Do not attempt to encode long sentences in an emergency — stick to SOS or very short messages (HELP, HERE).
red_flags: Inability to signal due to darkness, injury, or equipment failure with no rescue team in sight.
Battery depletion before rescue arrives with no alternative signaling method available.
preparedness_tips: A dash (−) lasts three times as long as a dot (·).
Space between signals within the same letter = 1 dot duration.

MEMORIZATION TIP: Frequency-weighted design means common letters have short codes. E = · (one dot), T = − (one dash), I = · · (two dots), A = · − (two signals). Start by memorizing S (· · ·), O (− − −), and E (·).
PRINT AND LAMINATE: Keep a printed copy of this chart in your emergency kit, go-bag, or vehicle glove box — you may need it when you cannot access this app.

source_references.title: Recommendation ITU-R M.1677-1: International Morse Code
source_references.whyUseful: The definitive international standard defining every Morse code character and timing rule — primary source for the full A–Z and 0–9 tables.

source_references.title: Army Field Manual FM 21-76: Survival
source_references.whyUseful: Confirms Morse code as a core field signaling skill and identifies SOS as the primary distress call for survivor signaling in field conditions.

source_references.title: Morse Code
source_references.whyUseful: Comprehensive overview of Morse code history, design principles, and the frequency-weighted alphabet structure useful for memorization guidance.

notes: This is a comprehensive upgrade of the existing morse-code stub. The key addition is the full ITU-R M.1677-1 character table in preparednessTips, structured as individual lookup entries so users can scroll to any letter or number quickly. The guide intentionally omits prosigns (SK, AR, etc.) and radio CW procedures — those are out of scope for survival use. The SOS prosign is covered in detail in the companion sos-morse-code action card.
change_summary: Comprehensive upgrade from stub: added full ITU-R M.1677-1 A-Z and 0-9 Morse code character table in preparednessTips, rewrote title/summary/quickAnswer, added preferredAction/backupAction, upgraded sourceQuality from mixed to strong with ITU and Army FM 21-76 sources.
